content:
# Luke Chapter 9 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 97-99 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_009_page_001.jpg, LUK_009_page_002.jpg, LUK_009_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And having called together the twelve, he gave them power and authority over all the demons, and to heal diseases.

**2** And he sent them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the sick.

**3** And he said to them, "Take nothing for the journey, neither staff nor bag nor bread nor money; nor have two tunics apiece.

**4** And into whatever house you enter, remain there, and from there depart.

**5** And as many as do not receive you, when you go out from that city, shake off the dust from your feet as a testimony against them."

**6** And going out, they were passing through village by village, proclaiming the good news and healing everywhere.

**7** Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all that was happening, and he was greatly perplexed, because it was said by some that John had been raised from the dead,

**8** and by some that Elijah had appeared, and by others that a certain prophet of the ancients had risen.

**9** And Herod said, "John I myself beheaded; but who is this about whom I hear such things?" And he was seeking to see him.

**10** And the apostles, having returned, reported to him all that they had done. And he took them and withdrew privately to a city called Bethsaida.

**11** But the crowds, having learned of it, followed him. And having received them, he was speaking to them concerning the kingdom of God, and those who had need of healing he was curing.

**12** And the day began to decline. And the twelve, having come to him, said, "Send the crowd away, so that they may go into the surrounding villages and countryside and find lodging and provisions, for here we are in a desolate place."

**13** But he said to them, "You yourselves give them something to eat." And they said, "We have no more than five loaves and two fish — unless we ourselves should go and buy food for all this people."

**14** For there were about five thousand men. And he said to his disciples, "Make them recline in groups of about fifty each."

**15** And they did so, and made them all recline.

**16** And having taken the five loaves and the two fish, and having looked up to heaven, he blessed them and broke them and was giving them to the disciples to set before the crowd.

**17** And they ate and were all satisfied; and there was taken up what remained to them of the broken pieces — twelve baskets.

**18** And it came to pass, as he was praying alone, the disciples were with him, and he questioned them, saying, "Who do the crowds say that I am?"

**19** And they answered and said, "John the Baptist; but others, Elijah; and others, that a certain prophet of the ancients has risen."

**20** And he said to them, "But who do you say that I am?" And Peter answered and said, "The Christ of God."

**21** And he, having strictly warned them, commanded them to tell this to no one,

**22** saying, "It is necessary that the Son of Man suffer many things and be rejected by the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be killed, and on the third day be raised."

**23** And he was saying to all, "If anyone wishes to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily, and follow me.

**24** For whoever wishes to save his life shall lose it; but whoever loses his life for my sake, this one shall save it.

**25** For what is a man profited, having gained the whole world, but having lost or forfeited himself?

**26** For whoever is ashamed of me and of my words, of this one the Son of Man shall be ashamed when he comes in his glory and that of the Father and of the holy angels.

**27** But I tell you truly, there are some of those standing here who shall not taste death until they see the kingdom of God."

**28** And it came to pass, about eight days after these words, that he took Peter and John and James and went up on the mountain to pray.

**29** And it came to pass, as he was praying, the appearance of his face became different, and his clothing became white, flashing like lightning.

**30** And behold, two men were conversing with him, who were Moses and Elijah,

**31** who, having appeared in glory, were speaking of his departure, which he was about to fulfill in Jerusalem.

**32** Now Peter and those with him were weighed down with sleep; but having fully awakened, they saw his glory and the two men standing with him.

**33** And it came to pass, as they were departing from him, Peter said to Jesus, "Master, it is good for us to be here; and let us make three tabernacles, one for you and one for Moses and one for Elijah" — not knowing what he was saying.

**34** And as he was saying these things, a cloud came and was overshadowing them; and they were afraid as they entered into the cloud.

**35** And a voice came out of the cloud, saying, "This is my Son, the Chosen One; listen to him."

**36** And when the voice had come, Jesus was found alone. And they kept silent and reported to no one in those days anything of what they had seen.

**37** And it came to pass, on the next day, when they had come down from the mountain, a great crowd met him.

**38** And behold, a man from the crowd cried out, saying, "Teacher, I beg you to look upon my son, for he is my only child.

**39** And behold, a spirit seizes him, and he suddenly cries out, and it convulses him with foaming, and it departs from him with difficulty, bruising him.

**40** And I begged your disciples to cast it out, and they were not able."

**41** And Jesus answered and said, "O faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here."

**42** And while he was still approaching, the demon threw him down and convulsed him. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit and healed the boy and gave him back to his father.

**43** And all were astonished at the majesty of God. And while all were marveling at all the things which he was doing, he said to his disciples,

**44** "Let these words sink into your ears: for the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men."

**45** But they did not understand this saying, and it was hidden from them, so that they might not perceive it; and they were afraid to ask him about this saying.

**46** And an argument arose among them as to which of them might be the greatest.

**47** And Jesus, knowing the reasoning of their heart, took hold of a child and set him beside himself,

**48** and said to them, "Whoever receives this child in my name receives me; and whoever receives me receives the one who sent me. For the one who is least among all of you, this one is great."

**49** And John answered and said, "Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we were forbidding him, because he does not follow with us."

**50** And Jesus said to him, "Do not forbid him; for he who is not against you is for you."

**51** And it came to pass, when the days were being fulfilled for his taking up, that he set his face to go to Jerusalem.

**52** And he sent messengers before his face. And they went and entered into a village of the Samaritans, so as to make ready for him.

**53** And they did not receive him, because his face was set toward Jerusalem.

**54** And when his disciples James and John saw this, they said, "Lord, do you wish that we command fire to come down from heaven and consume them?"

**55** But he turned and rebuked them.

**56** And they went to another village.

**57** And as they were going along the road, someone said to him, "I will follow you wherever you go."

**58** And Jesus said to him, "The foxes have dens, and the birds of heaven have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head."

**59** And he said to another, "Follow me." But he said, "Lord, permit me first to go and bury my father."

**60** And he said to him, "Leave the dead to bury their own dead; but you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God."

**61** And another also said, "I will follow you, Lord; but first permit me to bid farewell to those in my house."

**62** And Jesus said to him, "No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The three Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 97-99) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 8:52 through Luke 9:18 across four columns. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 9:24 through 9:45. Page 3 covers approximately Luke 9:51 through Luke 10:13. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΤΟΥΣ ΔΩΔΕΚΑ (9:1, "the twelve" — without further specification)
- ΜΗΤΕ ΡΑΒΔΟΝ (9:3, "neither staff" — prohibiting even a staff, diverging from Mark 6:8)
- ΒΗΘΣΑΙΔΑ (9:10, "Bethsaida" — city name clearly present)
- ΤΟΝ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ (9:20, "the Christ of God" — Luke's distinctive Petrine confession)
- ΚΑΘ ΗΜΕΡΑΝ (9:23, "daily" — Luke's distinctive addition to the cross-bearing command)
- ΩΣΕΙ ΗΜΕΡΑΙ ΟΚΤΩ (9:28, "about eight days" — Luke's inclusive reckoning)
- ΠΕΤΡΟΝ ΚΑΙ ΙΩΑΝΝΗΝ ΚΑΙ ΙΑΚΩΒΟΝ (9:28, "Peter and John and James" — Lukan order, consistent with 8:51)
- ΕΤΕΡΟΝ (9:29, "different" — Luke avoids the term "transfigured")
- ΕΞΑΣΤΡΑΠΤΩΝ (9:29, "flashing like lightning" — uniquely Lukan)
- ΕΞΟΔΟΝ (9:31, "departure/exodus" — theologically loaded term)
- Ο ΕΚΛΕΛΕΓΜΕΝΟΣ (9:35, "the Chosen One" — significant variant against Byzantine "the Beloved")
- ΑΝΑΛΗΜΨΕΩΣ (9:51, "taking up" — encompassing the entire passion-ascension complex)
- Shorter readings at 9:54-56 (no "as Elijah did," no extended rebuke speech)

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **9:2 — "the sick" vs. "the ones being sick":** EMET renders "the ones being sick," LOGOS and PNEUMA "the sick." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the sick" is natural English while preserving the substantive participle's meaning.

2. **9:3 — "money" vs. "silver":** EMET renders "silver" (literal for argyrion), LOGOS "money," PNEUMA "money." SOPHIA follows LOGOS (2-to-1) — "money" conveys the functional meaning; the material ("silver") is secondary.

3. **9:6 — "proclaiming the good news":** EMET renders "proclaiming-good-news" (hyphenated compound), LOGOS "proclaiming the good news," PNEUMA "announcing the good news." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — clear, dignified, and faithful to the single Greek verb euangelizomenoi.

4. **9:11 — "having learned of it" vs. "having known":** EMET renders "having known," LOGOS "having learned of it," PNEUMA "found out." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "having learned of it" conveys the aorist participle naturally while preserving participial structure.

5. **9:17 — "broken pieces" vs. "fragments":** EMET renders "fragments," LOGOS "broken pieces," PNEUMA "leftover pieces." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "broken pieces" renders klasmaton accurately without the slightly clinical tone of "fragments."

6. **9:25 — "lost" vs. "destroyed":** EMET renders "having destroyed," LOGOS "having lost," PNEUMA "lose." The Greek apolesas can mean either "destroy" or "lose." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "lost" pairs naturally with "forfeited" and suits the context of self-ruin.

7. **9:33 — "tabernacles" vs. "tents" vs. "shelters":** EMET renders "tents," LOGOS "tabernacles," PNEUMA "shelters." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "tabernacles" preserves the liturgical resonance of skenas, evoking the Feast of Tabernacles and the wilderness tabernacle, consistent with the theological density of the Transfiguration scene.

8. **9:35 — "listen to him" vs. "hear him":** EMET renders "hear him," LOGOS "listen to him," PNEUMA "Listen to him!" The Greek akouete autou carries the force of "listen to" (with obedient attention). SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "listen to him" is clearer in modern English than "hear him" for conveying the imperative of obedient attention.

9. **9:38 — "only child" vs. "only-born" vs. "only-begotten":** EMET renders "only-born," LOGOS "only-begotten," PNEUMA "only child." SOPHIA renders "only child" — consistent with the decision at 8:42 to render monogenes as "only" in narrative contexts describing human children.

10. **9:41 — "perverse" vs. "perverted":** EMET renders "having been perverted," LOGOS "perverse," PNEUMA "perverse." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "perverse" is an established biblical term for diestrammenē that preserves the Deuteronomy 32:5 echo without the awkwardness of "having been perverted."

11. **9:44 — "sink into your ears" vs. "place into your ears":** EMET renders literally "place into your ears," LOGOS renders "sink into your ears," PNEUMA "sink deep into your ears." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "let these words sink into your ears" communicates the Semitic idiom (thesthe eis ta ota) effectively while maintaining dignity. EMET's over-literal rendering sounds unnatural; PNEUMA's "deep" is an addition not in the Greek.

12. **9:46 — "argument" vs. "reasoning":** EMET renders "a reasoning entered among them," LOGOS "an argument arose among them," PNEUMA "an argument arose among the disciples." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — dialogismos here denotes an internal dispute or debate; "argument" captures this better than EMET's overly neutral "reasoning."

13. **9:47 — "child" vs. "little child":** EMET renders "little child," LOGOS "child," PNEUMA "little child." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the Greek paidion is simply "child"; the diminutive force is inherent in the word and need not be amplified with "little."

14. **9:48 — "this child" vs. "this little child":** Same principle as above. SOPHIA follows LOGOS for consistency.

15. **9:51 — "his taking up" rendering:** All three agents agree on the concept, differing only in phrasing. EMET: "being-taken-up," LOGOS: "his taking up," PNEUMA: "for him to be taken up." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "his taking up" is a clean nominal phrase faithful to the Greek noun analēmpseōs.

16. **9:53 — "was set toward" vs. "was going into":** EMET renders "was going into Jerusalem," LOGOS "was set to go to Jerusalem," PNEUMA "was heading toward Jerusalem." SOPHIA renders "was set toward Jerusalem" — blending LOGOS's "set" (preserving the echo of v. 51's "set his face") with directional clarity.

17. **9:58 — "birds of heaven" vs. "birds of the sky":** EMET and LOGOS render "birds of heaven"; PNEUMA "birds of the sky." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "of heaven" preserves the Greek tou ouranou, consistent with SOPHIA's rendering throughout prior chapters.

18. **9:62 — "plow" vs. "plough":** EMET renders "plow," LOGOS "plough" (British spelling), PNEUMA "plow." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "plow" is the standard American English spelling, consistent with SOPHIA's register throughout.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 9:2 | "the sick" rendering | "the [ones] being sick" | "the sick" | "the sick" | "the sick" | 2-to-1; natural English; substantive meaning preserved |
| 9:3 | "silver" vs. "money" | "silver" | "money" | "money" | "money" | 2-to-1; functional meaning of *argyrion* in context |
| 9:5 | Conditional form | "as many as might not receive" | "as many as do not receive" | "If people do not welcome" | "as many as do not receive" | LOGOS balances fidelity with clarity; PNEUMA too free |
| 9:6 | Verb for evangelizing | "proclaiming-good-news" | "proclaiming the good news" | "announcing the good news" | "proclaiming the good news" | Natural rendering of *euangelizomenoi*; no hyphenation needed |
| 9:7 | Degree of perplexity | "he was perplexed" | "he was greatly perplexed" | "he was deeply troubled" | "he was greatly perplexed" | LOGOS adds "greatly" to capture the force of imperfect *diēporei*; PNEUMA's "troubled" shifts meaning |
| 9:8 | "ancients" vs. "ancient ones" | "ancient [ones]" | "ancients" | "ancient prophets" | "ancients" | LOGOS is concise; PNEUMA adds "prophets" not in the Greek |
| 9:10 | "reported" vs. "recounted" | "recounted" | "reported" | "reported" | "reported" | 2-to-1; "reported" suits *diēgēsanto* in this context |
| 9:12 | "lodge" vs. "find lodging" | "lodge and find provisions" | "find lodging and provisions" | "find food and lodging" | "find lodging and provisions" | LOGOS phrasing is the most natural |
| 9:17 | Leftover description | "twelve baskets of fragments" | "twelve baskets" of "broken pieces" | "twelve baskets of leftover pieces" | "twelve baskets" of broken pieces | LOGOS's "broken pieces" is precise for *klasmaton* |
| 9:20 | Question form | "who do you say me to be?" | "who do you say that I am?" | "Who do you say I am?" | "who do you say that I am?" | LOGOS preserves formality; EMET is overly literal word order |
| 9:25 | "destroyed" vs. "lost" | "having destroyed" | "having lost" | "lose" | "having lost" | *apolesas* can mean both; "lost" pairs naturally with "forfeited" |
| 9:29 | Garment description | "white, flashing like lightning" | "white, flashing like lightning" | "dazzling white, blazing like lightning" | "white, flashing like lightning" | 2-to-1; PNEUMA's "dazzling" and "blazing" add words not in source |
| 9:32 | Sleep description | "were having been weighed down with sleep" | "were weighed down with sleep" | "were weighed down with sleep" | "were weighed down with sleep" | 2-to-1; EMET's periphrastic is awkward in English |
| 9:33 | "tabernacles" vs. "tents" | "tents" | "tabernacles" | "shelters" | "tabernacles" | Preserves liturgical/typological resonance of *skēnas* |
| 9:35 | "hear" vs. "listen to" | "hear him" | "listen to him" | "Listen to him!" | "listen to him" | "Listen to" conveys the obedient attention of *akouete* better |
| 9:38 | *monogenēs* rendering | "only-born" | "only-begotten" | "only child" | "only child" | Consistent with 8:42 SOPHIA decision; narrative context, not Johannine theology |
| 9:39 | "crushing" vs. "bruising" vs. "mauls" | "crushing him" | "bruising him" | "It mauls him" | "bruising him" | LOGOS's "bruising" for *syntribon* is precise; PNEUMA's "mauls" is too free |
| 9:41 | "faithless and perverted" | "faithless and having been perverted" | "faithless and perverse" | "unbelieving and perverse" | "faithless and perverse" | LOGOS wording is established biblical phrasing; EMET is awkward |
| 9:42 | "convulsed" detail | "convulsed [him]" | "convulsed him violently" | "threw him to the ground in a convulsion" | "convulsed him" | EMET and LOGOS agree on the verb; LOGOS adds "violently" not explicit in Greek |
| 9:44 | Ear idiom | "place into your ears" | "Let these words sink into your ears" | "Let these words sink deep into your ears" | "Let these words sink into your ears" | LOGOS captures the Semitic idiom naturally; EMET is over-literal |
| 9:46 | *dialogismos* rendering | "a reasoning entered among them" | "an argument arose among them" | "An argument arose among the disciples" | "an argument arose among them" | LOGOS captures the disputative sense; PNEUMA adds "disciples" |
| 9:47 | "little child" vs. "child" | "little child" | "child" | "little child" | "child" | Diminutive is inherent in *paidion*; no need to amplify |
| 9:48 | "receives" vs. "welcomes" | "receives" | "receives" | "welcomes" | "receives" | 2-to-1; *dechomai* = "receive"; "welcome" is interpretive expansion |
| 9:49 | Exorcist description | "someone in your name casting out" | "someone casting out demons in your name" | "someone driving out demons in your name" | "someone casting out demons in your name" | LOGOS word order is clearer; "casting out" is standard for *ekballonta* |
| 9:51 | *analēmpseōs* rendering | "being-taken-up" | "his taking up" | "for him to be taken up" | "his taking up" | LOGOS is a clean nominal form; EMET hyphenates awkwardly |
| 9:53 | Direction toward Jerusalem | "his face was going into Jerusalem" | "his face was set to go to Jerusalem" | "he was heading toward Jerusalem" | "his face was set toward Jerusalem" | Blends LOGOS's "set" (echoing v.51) with directional clarity |
| 9:54 | "tell fire" vs. "command fire" | "tell fire to come down" | "command fire to come down" | "call fire down" | "command fire to come down" | LOGOS's "command" better renders the authority implied in *eipōmen* here |
| 9:58 | "birds of heaven" vs. "sky" | "birds of the heaven" | "birds of heaven" | "birds of the sky" | "birds of heaven" | 2-to-1; consistent with prior SOPHIA renderings of *tou ouranou* |
| 9:59 | "permit me" vs. "let me" | "permit me, having gone away first" | "permit me first to go" | "first let me go" | "permit me first to go" | LOGOS is clear and faithful; PNEUMA's "let me" is slightly informal |
| 9:60 | "having gone away, proclaim" vs. "go and proclaim" | "having gone away, proclaim" | "go and proclaim" | "go and proclaim" | "go and proclaim" | 2-to-1; LOGOS is more natural English; participial is awkward here |
| 9:61 | "say farewell" vs. "bid farewell" | "say farewell" | "bid farewell" | "say goodbye" | "bid farewell" | LOGOS's "bid farewell" is the traditional rendering; PNEUMA's "say goodbye" is too informal |
| 9:62 | "plow" vs. "plough" | "plow" | "plough" | "plow" | "plow" | 2-to-1; American English standard consistent with SOPHIA register |
| 9:62 | "looking to the things behind" vs. "looks back" | "looking to the [things] behind" | "looks back" | "looks back" | "looks back" | 2-to-1; "looks back" is clear and natural; EMET is over-literal |

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
